reports.raw_content:
March 24, 2026                    CERTIFIED MAIL

Fatima Saleem Womack, Authorized Agent

Get It Right, Inc

21965 Finley Court N

Forest Lake, MN 55025

License Number: 1125400

ORDER OF LICENSE REVOCATION

Dear Fatima Saleem Womack:

The Department of Human Services (DHS) is revoking your license to provide children’s shelter services under Minnesota Rule 2960, Children’s Residential Facilities at 1130 Beech Street, Saint Paul, MN 55106. This revocation is based on disqualification of a controlling individual. Details of our findings are provided below. Our next steps and your options are also detailed.

The revocation goes into effect on March 30, 2026 at 5:00pm to allow time for delivery of this order and ten days for you to inform the Commissioner whether you intend to appeal the license revocation explained below.

REASON FOR LICENSE REVOCATION

1.  Disqualification of a controlling individual

On December 24, 2025, DHS notified you that a controlling individual (C1) had a disqualification and informed you that this person was disqualified from having direct contact with or access to persons receiving services from a licensed children’s residential facility. This includes access to the physical site location at 1130 Beech St. Saint Paul, MN 55106.

On January 26, 2026, C1 requested reconsideration of the disqualification. C1 has a disqualification that cannot be set aside and for which a variance cannot be granted. On March 10, 2026, DHS notified you and C1 that the disqualification was affirmed. If you appeal this revocation order, the issue of whether C1 poses a risk of harm to youth served by the program will not be reviewed as a part of the contested case hearing.

Legal Authority: Minnesota Statutes, section 245A.07, subdivision 3(a)(2).

Due to the serious nature of this violation, which impacts the health and safety of youth served in your care, your license to provide shelter services is revoked.

YOUR RIGHT TO APPEAL

You have the right to appeal the revocation. Your request must be in writing and clearly state that you are requesting a contested case hearing for this matter. Your request must be made before the deadlines provided below. If you do not meet this deadline, you lose your right to an administrative appeal. The timeline to appeal began when you received this order.

If you are mailing your request, it must be sent by certified mail and postmarked within 10 calendar days from when you received this order. Please send it to:

Commissioner, Department of Human Services

Office of Inspector General

Legal Counsel’s Office

Attention: Licensing Legal Unit

PO Box 64953

St. Paul, MN 55164-0953

If your request is being personally delivered, it must be received by DHS within 10 calendar days from when you received this order. Please bring it to:

Commissioner, Department of Human Services

Office of Inspector General

Legal Counsel’s Office

Attention: Licensing Legal Unit

444 Lafayette Road North

St. Paul, MN 55155

Upon DHS’ receipt of your timely appeal, your case would be scheduled for a contested case hearing in front of an Administrative Law Judge. Following this hearing, the Commissioner of DHS will issue a final order. If you do not appeal or if the order is affirmed by the Commissioner following a hearing, DHS is prohibited from issuing you and the controlling individuals a license for five years. In addition, any additional licenses held by you or the controlling individuals shall also be revoked.

Legal representation at the contested case hearing:

You do not need a lawyer to appeal. However, a lawyer can help you with your appeal. The state or county will not get you a lawyer and will not pay for a lawyer. If you cannot afford a lawyer, you may be able to get free legal advice or help with your appeal. To find out if free help is available, contact: Volunteer Lawyers Network at 612-752-6677; Central Minnesota Legal Services at 612-332-8151; Southern Minnesota Legal Services at 651-222-4731; or go to www.lawhelpmn.org to find a local legal services program that may be able to help you.

You can also find information on contested cases from the Office of Administrative Hearings website at https://mn.gov/oah/self-help. Click on Administrative Law Overview, then click on Administrative Law Contested Case Hearing Guide for a list of frequently asked questions.

Operating the program pending the outcome of the appeal:

If you file an appeal within the timeframes described above, you may continue to operate pending the outcome of your appeal. If you continue to operate, you must comply with the conditions described below. If you continue to operate, you must do so in full compliance with all licensing laws and rules. Failure to follow a law or rule that may impact the health or safety of youths served by your program could result in the immediate suspension of your license.

If you continue to operate pending the outcome of your appeal, you must comply with the following conditions:

1. C1 is disqualified from having direct contact with or access to persons served by Get It Right, Inc. and shall not have access to such persons’ financial, personal or health information or physical location at 1130 Beech St. Saint Paul, MN 55106.

Legal authority for this licensing action

1. This action is taken under Minnesota Statutes, section 245A.07, subdivision 3, which describes the conditions under which DHS may revoke a license.

2. The timeline to appeal a revocation order is provided in Minnesota Statutes, section 245A.07, subdivision 3(b).

3. “Controlling individual” is defined under Minnesota Statutes, section 245A.02, subdivision 5a.

4. When a revocation of a license is based on a disqualification that cannot be set aside, the scope of the contested case hearing for the revocation shall not include a review of whether the individual poses a risk of harm to persons served by the program. Minnesota Statutes, section 245A.08, subdivision 2a, paragraph (g), and Minnesota Statutes, section 245C.24, subdivision 2(a).

5. Minnesota Statutes, section 245.095 defines which programs administered by DHS are included in the exclusion provision, and further defines “excluded,” “individual,” and “provider.”

6. License holders have a right to appeal licensing actions and request a contested case hearing, under Minnesota Statutes, chapter 14 and Minnesota Rules, parts 1400.8505 to 1400.8612.

7. If a license holder files a timely appeal of a revocation order, the license holder may continue to operate the program pending a final order of the appeal under Minnesota Statutes, section 245A.07, subdivision 1(b).

8. Under Minnesota Statutes, section 245A.04, subdivision 7, paragraph (d), clause (3), the commissioner shall not issue or reissue a license if the applicant, license holder, or controlling individual has had a license issued under this chapter revoked within the past five years.

9. Under Minnesota Statutes, section 245A.04, subdivision 7, paragraph (d), clause (5), when a license issued under this chapter is revoked under clause (1) or (3), the license holder and controlling individual may not hold any license under chapter 245A for five years following the revocation, and other licenses held by the applicant, license holder, or controlling individual shall also be revoked.

10. Under Minnesota Statutes, section 245A.07, subdivision 1, paragraph (b), the commissioner may include terms the license holder must follow pending a final order on appeal.

Questions

11. If you have any further questions regarding this matter, you may contact Paula Halverson, Supervisor, at 651-431-5653.

Sincerely,

Paula Halverson, Unit Manager

Licensing Division

Office of Inspector General

PO Box 64242 • Saint Paul, Minnesota • 55164-0242 • An Equal Opportunity and Veteran Friendly Employer

reports.raw_content:
January 24, 2025                  CERTIFIED MAIL

Tony Sanders, Authorized Agent

Grafton Integrated Health Network Minnesota

3150 Shawnee Dr.

Winchester, VA 22601

License Number: 1109203 (PRTF)

ORDER TO PAY A FINE FOR

BACKGROUND STUDY VIOLATION

Dear Tony Sanders:

The Department of Human Services (DHS) is ordering you to pay a fine of $200 for 2 background study violations. Details of our findings are provided below. Our next steps and your options are also detailed.

Reason for the Fine

DHS is issuing this fine order based on your failure to comply with the following background study requirement:

Background Study Violation Determined on December 2, 2024:

1. As a result of a licensing review, a DHS licensor determined that you failed to request a background study (BGS) for two staff persons (SP1 and SP2) before an adult, regardless of whether the individual will have direct contact with persons served by the children’s residential facility, began working in the facility.

a. SP1 began working in a children’s residential facility on February 26, 2024: however, the license holder did not submit a BGS request to DHS for SP1 until April 24, 2024.

Under Minnesota Statutes, section 245A.07, subdivision 3, paragraph (d), except for BGS violations involving the failure to comply with an order to immediately remove an individual or an order to provide continuous, direct supervision, the Commissioner shall not issue a fine to a license holder who self-corrects a BGS violation before the commissioner discovers the violation. A license holder who has previously exercised the provisions of this paragraph to avoid a fine for a BGS violation may not avoid a fine for a subsequent BGS violation unless at least 365 days have passed since the license holder self-corrected the earlier BGS violations.

Because the license holder self-corrected the background study violation involving SP1 before the Commissioner discovered the violation, the license holder is not being fined for the background study violation involving SP1.

b. SP2 began working in a children’s residential facility on July 15, 2024. At the time of the licensing review on December 2, 2024, the license holder had not submitted a background study request to DHS for SP2.

Because the license holder allowed SP2 to work in a children’s residential facility without submitting a background study request, the license holder is being fined for the background study violation involving SP2.

Statute Violated: Minnesota Statutes 245C.03, subdivision 1 and 245C.04, subdivision 11.

Fine: $200 (one violation at $200 per violation)

Corrective Action Required: You must comply with background study requirements in Minnesota Statutes, chapter 245C. Within 10 days from receipt of this order, submit written documentation to your licensor detailing how compliance will be maintained in the future.

Billing and Payment of the Fine

DHS will send you an invoice for the $200 fine. Payment must be made as directed on the invoice.

If you request a contested case hearing, as described below, do not pay the fine at this time. Following the contested case hearing, the Commissioner of DHS will issue a final order on this matter.

Please note, you may not avoid payment of this fine by closing, selling, or otherwise transferring the license to a third party. If this occurs, you are personally and jointly responsible for payment. If you do not pay the fine on or before the date specified on the invoice and you did not request a contested case hearing, as described below, the Commissioner may issue a second fine, may not issue or reissue a license, or may suspend the license until the license holder pays the fine.

Your Right to Appeal

You have the right to appeal the fine. Your request must be in writing and clearly state that you are requesting a contested case hearing for this matter. Your request must be made before the deadlines provided below. If you do not meet this deadline, you lose your right to an administrative appeal. The timeline to appeal began when you received this order.

If you are mailing your request, it must be sent by certified mail and postmarked within 10 calendar days from when you receive this order. Please send it to:

Commissioner, Department of Human Services

Office of Inspector General

Legal Counsel’s Office

Attn: Licensing Legal Unit

PO Box 64953

St. Paul, MN 55164-0953

If your request is being personally delivered, it must be received by DHS within 10 calendar days from when you receive this order. Please bring it to:

Commissioner, Department of Human Services

Office of Inspector General

Legal Counsel’s Office

Attn: Licensing Legal Unit

444 Lafayette Road North

St. Paul, MN 55155

Upon DHS’ receipt of your timely appeal, your case would be scheduled for a contested case hearing in front of an Administrative Law Judge. Following this hearing, the Commissioner of DHS will issue a final order.

Legal representation at the contested case hearing

You do not need a lawyer to appeal. However, a lawyer can help you with your appeal. The state or county will not get you a lawyer and will not pay for a lawyer. If you cannot afford a lawyer, you may be able to get free legal advice or help with your appeal. To find out if free help is available, contact: Volunteer Lawyers Network at 612-752-6677; Central Minnesota Legal Services at 612-332-8151; Southern Minnesota Legal Services at 651-222-4731; or go to www.lawhelpmn.org to find a local legal services program that may be able to help you.

You can also find information on contested cases from the Office of Administrative Hearings website at https://mn.gov/oah/self-help. Click on Administrative Law Overview, then click on Administrative Law Contested Case Hearing Guide for a list of frequently asked questions.

Legal Authority for this Licensing Action

· DHS may impose a $200 fine for each background study violation under Minnesota Statutes, section 245A.07, subdivision 3, paragraph (c)(4)(iv).

· Minnesota Statutes, section 245A.07, subdivision 3, paragraph (c)(5) states that license holders may not avoid payment of a fine by closing, selling, or transferring a license.

· Minnesota Statutes, section 245A.04, subdivision 7, paragraph (d)(4), states that DHS shall not issue or reissue a license if the applicant, license holder, or controlling individual has an outstanding debt related to a license fee, licensing fine, or settlement agreement for which payment is delinquent.

· Minnesota Statutes, section 245A.07, subdivision 3, paragraph (c)(2), states that that the license holder shall pay the fine assessed on or before the payment date specified, and if the license holder does not do so the commissioner may issue a second fine or suspend the license until the license holder complies.

· License holders have a right to appeal licensing actions and request a contested case hearing, under Minnesota Statutes, chapter 14 and Minnesota Rules, parts 1400.8505 to 1400.8612.

Questions

If you have any further questions regarding this matter, you may contact Katie Leuer, Supervisor, at 651-431-6259.

Sincerely,

Paula Halverson, Unit Manager

Licensing Division

Office of Inspector General

PO Box 64242 • Saint Paul, Minnesota • 55164-0242 • An Equal Opportunity and Veteran Friendly Employer

reports.raw_content:
January 24, 2025

Tony Sanders, Authorized Agent

Grafton Integrated Health Network Minnesota

3150 Shawnee Dr.

Winchester, VA 22601

License Number: 1109203 (PRTF)

Report Number: 202500013

CORRECTION ORDER

Dear Tony Sanders:

On November 18 and 19, 2024, and December 2 through 4, 2024, Department of Human Services (DHS) licensors conducted a licensing review and investigation at your facility, Grafton Integrated Health Network Minnesota, located at 224 Kray Mill Rd., Cold Spring, MN 56320. This review was conducted to determine compliance with state and federal laws and rules governing the provision of psychiatric residential treatment facilities under the variance to Minnesota Rules, Chapter 2960; R2960V. As a result, DHS is issuing this order which requires you to take the correction action as described under each violation. Details of our findings are provided below. Our next steps and your options are also detailed.

LICENSING VIOLATIONS

DHS determined that your program failed to follow licensing rules and statutes, as described below.

Postings, Policies, and Practices

1. Violation: The license holder’s internal review of a report of alleged or suspected maltreatment of a minor did not meet requirements. Internal reviews for reports dated May 20, 2024, and October 23, 2024, did not include the following required components:

a. An evaluation of whether:

i. The policies and procedures were adequate;

ii. There was a need for additional staff training; and

iii. The reported event was similar to past events with the children or the services involved;

b. Based on the results of the internal review, documentation of whether the license holder developed, documented, and implemented a corrective action plan designed to correct current lapses and prevent future lapses in performance by individuals or the license holder; and

c. The date the internal review was completed.

Statute Violated: Minnesota Statues, 245A.66, subdivision 1.

Corrective Action Required: Immediately and on an ongoing basis, the license holder must ensure the completion of internal reviews meets all applicable requirements. Within 45 days receipt of this order, submit an internal review for each instance of alleged maltreatment identified above, that includes all required components to demonstrate compliance.

2. Violation: The license holder did not monitor the implementation of a corrective action plan following an incident on March 31, 2024. Documentation demonstrated that the additional training and weekly supervision that was required by the plan was not provided.

Statute Violated: Minnesota Statues, section 245A.04, subdivision 14, paragraph (b).

Corrective Action Required: Immediately and on an ongoing basis, the license holder must ensure resident’s rights are protected. See the corrective action ordered in violation number 1 to demonstrate compliance.

3. Violation: During the onsite licensing review and investigation, the license holder did not provide the commissioner with timely and complete access to personnel files to demonstrate compliance with applicable statutes and rules. The commissioner must be given access without prior notice and as often as the commissioner considers necessary if the commissioner is conducting a licensing inspection.

Statute Violated: Minnesota Statutes, section 245A.04, subdivision 5, paragraph (a).

Repeat Violation: In a Correction Order that DHS issued on July 25, 2024, you were previously found in violation of this same statute.

Corrective Action Required: Immediately and on an ongoing basis, the license holder must ensure the commissioner’s right of access meet all applicable requirements.

4. Violation: The license holder did not meet requirements governing staff ratios. For the three weeks reviewed (October 27 through November 16, 2024), documentation did not demonstrate the following:

a. That at least one staff person to every three residents present within the living unit was present during awake hours for the following dates:

i. October 27, 28, and 31, 2024, and November 1, 4, 5, 8, 12, and 13, 2024; and

b. That at least one staff person to every six residents present within the living unit was present during sleeping hours for the following dates:

i. October 28 and 29, 2024, and November 3 through 6, 10 through 12, 15, and 16, 2024.

Rule Violated: Minnesota Variance, section R2960V.14, subparts 2 and 3.

Repeat Violation: In a Correction Order that DHS issued on July 25, 2024, you were previously found in violation of this same rule.

Corrective Action Required: Immediately and on an ongoing basis, the license holder must ensure the provision of staff ratios meet all applicable requirements. Within 45 days receipt of this order, submit two consecutive weeks of unit staff schedules and the corresponding daily resident census per each living unit that demonstrates compliance.

5. Violation: The license holder did not meet requirements governing clinical supervision. For two of two weeks reviewed, the license holder did not ensure all treatment team members participated either in clinical supervision or an ancillary meeting for each calendar week they worked, for the weeks of:

a. September 9, 2024, when seven staff persons did not attend; and

b. October 14, 2024, when six staff persons did not attend.

Rule Violated: Minnesota Variance, section R2960V.13.

Repeat Violation: Violations of this same requirement were previously found in Correction Orders that DHS issued on:

· July 25, 2024

· February 27, 2023

Corrective Action Required: Immediately and on an ongoing basis, the license holder must ensure clinical supervision for program staff meets all applicable requirements.

6. Violation: The license holder did not meet requirements governing quality assurance and improvement. The license holder did not complete a quarterly review that considered the following required components (Quarters 2 and 3 of 2024):

a. Resident outcomes, including identifying ways to improve effectiveness of the services provided to residents and to improve resident outcomes;

b. Restraint data, according to part R2960V.11, subpart 5;

c. Serious occurrences and other significant incidents, including:

i. Determining whether policies and procedures were followed;

ii. Evaluating the staff’s response to the serious occurrence and other significant incidents;

iii. Assessing what could have prevented the serious occurrence and other significant incidents from occurring; and

iv. Modifying policies, procedures, training plans, or resident’s ITPs in response to the findings of the review; and

d. Self-monitoring of compliance, including evaluating compliance with the requirements of the PRTF Variance, R2960V, and demonstrating action to improve the program’s compliance with the requirements.

Rule Violated: Minnesota Variance, section R2960V.17, subpart 1.

Repeat Violation: Violations of this same requirement were previously found in Correction Orders that DHS issued on:

· July 25, 2024

· February 27, 2023

Corrective Action Required: Immediately and on an ongoing basis, the license holder must ensure the quality assurance and improvement plan meets all applicable requirements. Within 45 days receipt of this order, submit the 2024 Quarter 4 review that demonstrates compliance.

Personnel Files

7. Violation: Ten of ten personnel files reviewed for requirements governing training for staff providing services to individuals who qualify for the Positive Support Rule (PSR) did not meet requirements in the following ways:

a. For staff persons responsible for developing, implementing, monitoring, supervising, and/or evaluating positive support strategies, a positive support transition plan, and/or the emergency use of manual restraint, training documentation demonstrated the following:

i. There was no training completed (personnel file numbered 8); and

ii. At least eight hours of core training was not completed (personnel files numbered 1 through 7, and 9 through 10);

iii. The following required Core Training topics were not completed:

1. Staff responsibilities related to restricted and permitted actions (personnel files numbered 1 and 9);

2. The situations in which staff must contact 911 services in response to an imminent risk of harm to the persons or others (personnel files numbered 1 through 7, and 9 through 10); and

3. Personal staff accountability and staff self-care after emergencies (personnel files numbered 1 through 7, and 9 through 10);

b. For staff persons who develop positive support strategies and program managers, an additional four hours of function-specific training based upon the level of responsibility and qualifications, was not completed prior to assuming responsibilities, to the following required topics:

i. Functional behavior assessments (personnel files numbered 2 and 8 through 10);

ii. How to apply person-centered planning (personnel files numbered 1, 2, and 8);

iii. How to design and use data systems to measure effectiveness of care (personnel files numbered 1, 2, and 8 through 10); and

iv. Supervision, including how to train, coach, and evaluate staff and encourage effective communication with the resident and the resident’s support team (personnel files numbered 1, 2, and 8 through 10); and

c. For staff persons in manager positions, a minimum of an additional two hours of function-specific training to the following required topics was not completed (personnel files numbered 1, 2, and 8 through 10):

i. How to include staff in organizational decisions;

ii. Management of the organization based upon person-centered thinking and practices and how to address person centered thinking and practices in the organization; and

iii. Evaluation of organizational training as it applies to the measurement of behavior change and improved outcomes for persons receiving services.

Rule Violated: Minnesota Rules, part 9544.0090, subparts 1 and 2.

Repeat Violation: In a Correction Order that DHS issued on July 25, 2024, you were previously found in violation of this same rule.

Corrective Action Required: Immediately and on an ongoing basis, the license holder must ensure the provision of staff training for PSR meets all applicable requirements. Within 45 days receipt of this order, submit training rosters demonstrating the staff persons identified above have completed the required training.

8. Violation: One of ten personnel files reviewed for requirements governing the provision of background studies (personnel file numbered 5) did not meet requirements. The license holder failed to either initiate a new background study for an individual following a legal name change or did not notify the background studies division of the legal name change.

Statute Violated: Minnesota Statutes, sections 245C.04, subdivision 7.

Corrective Action Ordered: Immediately and on an ongoing basis, the license holder must ensure background studies meet all applicable requirements. Within 45 days receipt of this order, submit documentation demonstrating the updated background study has been completed for the staff person identified above.

9. Violation: Seven of ten personnel files reviewed for requirements governing staff files and background study subjects did not meet requirements. The files did not include:

a. The date the background study subject/staff person had direct contact with persons served by the license holder’s program (personnel file numbered 10);

b. Documentation of orientation (personnel files numbered 1 and 8);

c. An annual job performance evaluation (personnel files numbered 4, 8, and 9);

d. An annual development and training plan (personnel files numbered 4, 5, and 7 through 10); and

e. Records of training and education activities that were completed during employment (personnel file numbered 8).

Statute and Rule Violated: Minnesota Statutes, section 245A.041, subdivision 6, and Minnesota Variance, section R2960V.15, subpart 5.

Repeat Violation: Violations of this same requirement were previously found in Correction Orders that DHS issued on:

· July 25, 2024

· February 27, 2023

Corrective Action Required: Immediately and on an ongoing basis, the license holder must ensure the inclusion of staff files and background study requirements meet all applicable requirements.

10. Violation: Four of five personnel files reviewed for requirements governing orientation training did not meet requirements in the following ways:

a. The staff person did not receive orientation to the following prior to direct contact:

i. The vulnerable adult maltreatment reporting requirements and definitions in Minnesota Statutes, sections 245A.65, subdivision 3, 626.557, and 626.5572, and all internal policies and procedures related to the prevention and reporting of vulnerable adults (personnel files numbered 1 and 8);

ii. The license holder’s program abuse prevention plan (PAPP) (personnel files numbered 1 through 3, 6, and 8);

iii. The child maltreatment reporting requirements in Minnesota Statutes, section 260E (personnel files numbered 1 and 8);

iv. Resident Rights (personnel files numbered 1 and 8);

v. Emergency procedures (personnel files numbered 1 and 8);

vi. Resiliency and recovery concepts and principles (personnel files numbered 1 and 8);

vii. Gender based needs (personnel files numbered 1 and 8);

viii. Resident confidentiality (personnel files numbered 1 and 8); and

ix. Training related to the specific activities and job functions that the staff person will be responsible to carry out (personnel files numbered 1, 2, 6, and 8); and

b. The staff person did not receive orientation to the following within 30 calendar days of direct contact (personnel files numbered 1 and 8):

i. Facility policies and procedures;

ii. Treatment needs of residents, including psychiatric disorders and co-occurring disorders; and

iii. Best practice service delivery including:

1. Trauma Informed Care;

2. Developmentally appropriate care;

3. The characteristics, and treatment of residents with special needs such as substance abuse, obsessive compulsive disorder, and eating disorders; and

4. Co-occurring disorders as defined by the population being served.

Rule Violated: Minnesota Variance, section R2960V.16, subpart 2.

Repeat Violation: Violations of this same requirement were previously found in Correction Orders that DHS issued on:

· July 25, 2024

· February 27, 2023

Corrective Action Required: Immediately and on an ongoing basis, the license holder must ensure the provision of orientation training meets all applicable requirements. Within 45 days receipt of this order, submit training checklists related to the specific activities and job functions for clinical, supervisor, and administrative positions of the program to demonstrate compliance.

11. Violation: Five of five personnel files reviewed for requirements governing annual training did not meet requirements. The staff person did not receive training annually to the following:

a. The vulnerable adult maltreatment reporting requirements and definitions in Minnesota Statutes, sections 245A.65, subdivision 3, 626.557, and 626.5572, and all internal policies and procedures related to the prevention and reporting of vulnerable adults (personnel file numbered 8);

b. The license holder’s program abuse prevention plan (PAPP) (personnel files numbered 4, 5, and 7 through 10);

c. The child maltreatment reporting requirements in Minnesota Statutes, section 260E (personnel file numbered 8);

d. Resident rights (personnel files numbered 7 and 8);

e. Emergency procedures (personnel files numbered 7 and 8); and

f. Treatment services for residents with co-occurring disorders (personnel files numbered 4, 5, and 7 through 9).

Rule Violated: Minnesota Variance, section R2960V.16, subpart 3.

Repeat Violation: In a Correction Order that DHS issued on July 25, 2024, you were previously found in violation of this same rule.

Corrective Action Required: Immediately and on an ongoing basis, the license holder must ensure the provision of annual training meets all applicable requirements.

Resident Files

12. Violation: Seven of seven resident files reviewed for requirements governing the Positive Support Rule did not meet requirements in the following ways:

a. The license holder did not provide written notice to the resident and their legal representative, at the time-of-service initiation or when the license holder changed the policy about the emergency use of manual restraint (EUMR), of the EUMR policy that included the resident’s rights under the PSR and Minnesota Statutes 245D.04 (resident files numbered 1 through 7); and

b. The Functional Behavior Assessment (FBA) did not meet requirements in the following ways (resident files numbered 3 and 4):

i. The FBA was not conducted by a qualified professional;

ii. Direct observation of the resident by the evaluator was not included; and

iii. The FBA did not include an evaluation of the following elements:

1. Biological factors, identified through a medical or dental assessment;

2. Psychological factors, identified through a diagnostic assessment or suicidality assessment;

3. Environmental factors, identified through direct observation or interviewing a significant individual in the person’s life; and

4. Quality of life indicators based on the person’s goals and needs within the domains of a meaningful life.

Rule Violated: Minnesota Rules, parts 9544.0040, subparts 1 and 3, and 9544.0080.

Repeat Violation: In a Correction Order that DHS issued on July 25, 2024, you were previously found in violation of this same rule.

Corrective Action Required: Immediately and on an ongoing basis, the license holder must ensure the provision of PSR meets all applicable requirements. Within 30 days receipt of this order, submit an FBA or an FBA template that demonstrates compliance.

13. Violation: One of four resident files reviewed for requirements governing program rules (resident file numbered 2) did not meet requirements. There was no documentation to demonstrate that the license holder communicated the program rules and details for the due process system used in the facility, both verbally and in writing, to the resident.

Rule Violated: Minnesota Variance, section R2960V.10.

Repeat Violation: Violations of this same requirement were previously found in Correction Orders that DHS issued on:

· July 25, 2024

· February 27, 2023

Corrective Action Required: Immediately and on an ongoing basis, the license holder must ensure the provision of program rules meets all applicable requirements.

14. Violation: Seven of seven resident files reviewed for requirements governing the individual plan of care did not meet requirements in the following ways:

a. The immediate needs assessment and preliminary plan of care did not contain specific measures to minimize safety risks (resident file numbered 6);

b. Treatment objectives were not measurable and did not include target dates for achievement (resident files numbered 1 through 3 and 5 through 7);

c. Strategy interventions did not identify the staff who were responsible to deliver the interventions (resident files numbered 1 through 3 and 5 through 7);

d. Strategy interventions did not identify the frequency they were to be implemented (resident files numbered 1 through 3 and 5 through 7)

e. Medication management treatment goals were not reflected on the plan (resident files numbered 1 through 3 and 5 through 7);

f. Individual support plans were developed late for individuals that displayed behaviors that required the use of restraint (resident files numbered 3 and 4); and

g. The plan of care did not contain the resident and legal guardian’s signature to acknowledge their participation in the development and/or revisions of the plan of care (resident files numbered 1 through 3 and 5 through 7); and

h. The titles of the multidisciplinary team members were not included for those who completed and/or updated the plan of care (resident files numbered 1 through 3 and 5 through 7).

Rule Violated: Minnesota Variance, section R2960V.07, subpart 1.

Repeat Violation: Violations of this same requirement were previously found in Correction Orders that DHS issued on:

· July 25, 2024

· February 27, 2023

Corrective Action Required: Immediately and on an ongoing basis, the license holder must ensure the individual plan of care meets all applicable requirements. Within 45 days receipt of this order, submit an individual plan of care for two residents that demonstrates compliance.

15. Violation: Two of seven resident files reviewed for requirements governing medication reconciliation and medication administration did not meet requirements in the following ways:

a. There was no documentation to demonstrate that a medication reconciliation was completed upon admission (resident file numbered 2); and

b. There was no notation on the resident’s medication administration record to indicate if the medication was administered as prescribed (resident file numbered 6).

Rule Violated: Minnesota Variance, section R2960V.08, subparts 3 and 4, item B.

Repeat Violation: Violations of this same requirement were previously found in Correction Orders that DHS issued on:

· July 25, 2024

· February 27, 2023

Corrective Action Required: Immediately and on an ongoing basis, the license holder must ensure medication reconciliation meets all applicable requirements.

16. Violation: Seven of seven resident files reviewed for requirements governing psychotropic medication reviews did not meet requirements in the following ways:

a. The prescribing practitioner did not document a description in observable and measurable terms of the symptoms and behaviors the psychotropic medication is to alleviate (resident files numbered 1, 2, and 5 through 7);

b. The file did not contain documentation to demonstrate that the prescribing practitioner conducted and documented psychotropic medication reviews accordingly:

i. Weekly for the first month (resident file numbered 7); and

ii. Every month thereafter (resident files numbered 1 through 5); and

c. Resident file documentation did not demonstrate that the license holder considered the following for each psychotropic medication review conducted by the prescribing practitioner:

i. Targeted symptoms and behaviors of concern (resident files numbered 1 through 7);

ii. Data collected since the last review (resident filed numbered 3); and

iii. Side effects observed and actions taken (resident file numbered 6).

Rule Violated: Minnesota Variance, section R2960V.08, subpart 6, items B and C.

Repeat Violation: Violations of this same requirement were previously found in Correction Orders that DHS issued on:

· July 25, 2024

· February 27, 2023

Corrective Action Required: Immediately and on an ongoing basis, the license holder must ensure psychotropic medication reviews meet all applicable requirements. Within 45 days receipt of this order, submit psychotropic medication review documentation for two residents that demonstrates compliance.

17. Violation: Five of five resident files reviewed for requirements governing informed consent did not meet requirements in the following ways:

a. Informed consent was not obtained for an antipsychotic medication that was prescribed and administered to a resident (resident file numbered 5);

b. Documentation did not demonstrate the resident was informed and involved, if possible, in the decision making for each medication prescribed and administered (resident files numbered 5 and 6);

c. Informed consent documentation for prescribed psychotropic and antipsychotic medications did not contain the signature of the resident to acknowledge the prescribing practitioner or designee talked about the medication with the resident, answered questions, and that the resident agreed to the medication and dosage (resident files numbered 1, 2, 6, and 7).

Rule Violated: Minnesota Variance, section R2960V.08, subparts 7, item A, and 8.

Repeat Violation: Violations of this same requirement were previously found in Correction Orders that DHS issued on:

· July 25, 2024

· February 27, 2023

Corrective Action Required: Immediately and on an ongoing basis, the license holder must ensure informed consents meet all applicable requirements.

18. Violation: Seven of seven resident files reviewed for requirements governing medication side effect monitoring did not meet requirements in the following ways:

a. The license holder did not document and monitor for side effects within 24 hours of admission (resident files numbered 1, 2, and 7);

b. The license holder did not monitor for side effects weekly as determined by the prescribing psychiatric practitioner (resident files numbered 1 through 7); and

c. A standardized checklist or scale developed for a specific drug class as a monitoring tool was not identified for use as required by the prescribing psychiatric practitioner for residents prescribed antipsychotic medications (resident files numbered 1 through 7).

Rule Violated: Minnesota Variance, section R2960V.08, subparts 2, item G, and 10.

Corrective Action Required: Immediately and on an ongoing basis, the license holder must ensure the monitoring of medication side effects meet all applicable requirements. Within 45 days receipt of this order, submit the standardized checklist or scale that will be utilized to monitor the side effects for antipsychotic medications to demonstrate compliance.

Repeat Violation: In a Correction Order that DHS issued on July 25, 2024, you were previously found in violation of this same rule.

19. Violation: Two of two resident files reviewed for requirements governing the documentation of the use of restraint and seclusion did not meet requirements. The documentation did not demonstrate the following:

a. Prior events that may have been a contributing factor of the incident on July 27 and August 20, 2024 (resident file numbered 3) and on September 11 and October 26, 2024 (resident file numbered 4);

b. Why the supportive and less restrictive interventions failed or were found to be inappropriate on July 27 and August 7 and 20, 2024 (resident file numbered 3) and on September 11 and October 26, 2024 (resident file numbered 4); and

c. The types of interventions utilized including the type of physical holding used on September 11, 2024 (resident file numbered 4).

Rule Violated: Minnesota Variance, section R2960V.11, subpart 2.

Corrective Action Required: Immediately and on an ongoing basis, the license holder must ensure the documentation of use of restraint meets all applicable requirements. Within 45 days receipt of this order, submit a revised form or template for the documentation of uses of restraints to demonstrate compliance.

20. Violation: Two of two resident files reviewed for requirements governing debriefings following the use of restraint did not meet requirements in the following ways:

a. There was no documentation to demonstrate that a both debriefing sessions were conducted after the use of restraint for the following occurrences:

i. July 27 and August 7 and 20, 2024 (resident file numbered 3);

ii. September 11 and October 26, 2024 (resident file numbered 4); and

b. The debriefing documentation for incidents dated August 7 and 20, 2024 (resident file numbered 3) and September 11 and October 26, 2024 (resident file numbered 4) did not demonstrate the following

i. Whether any changes to the resident’s treatment plan or additional staff training resulted from the debriefings;

ii. Whether the license holder provided the resident with the opportunity to have a legal representative or advocate participate in the debriefing; and

iii. Whether the precipitating factors and alternative techniques that might have prevented the use of restraints were incorporated into the resident’s individual support plan to prevent future use.

Rule Violated: Minnesota Variance, section R2960V.11, subpart 3, items A through C.

Repeat Violation: Violations of this same requirement were previously found in Correction Orders that DHS issued on:

· July 25, 2024

· February 27, 2023

Corrective Action Required: Immediately and on an ongoing basis, the license holder must ensure the provision and documentation of debriefings meet all applicable requirements. Within 45 days receipt of this order, submit a revised form or template for the documentation of both staff and resident debriefing sessions that demonstrates compliance.

21. Violation: Two of two resident files reviewed for requirements governing administrative reviews of restrictive procedures did not meet requirements in the following ways:

a. There was no documentation to demonstrate an administrative review was completed for the use of restraint on July 27, 2024 (resident file numbered 3);

b. Documentation did not identify that the resident or the resident's representative had an opportunity to present evidence and argument to the reviewer about why the procedure was unwarranted, for the following uses of restraint:

i. August 7 and 20, 2024 (resident file numbered 3); and

ii. September 11 and October 26, 2024 (resident file numbered 4); and

c. The record of the administrative review for incidents dated August 7 and 20, 2024 (resident file numbered 3) and September 11 and October 26, 2024 (resident file numbered 4) did not include the following required components:

i. If the required documentation was recorded;

ii. If the restrictive procedure was used in accordance with the treatment plan;

iii. An evaluation of whether the rule standards governing the use of restrictive procedures were met; and

iv. Whether or not the staff who implemented the restrictive procedure were properly trained.

Rule Violated: Minnesota Variance, section R2960V.11, subpart 4.

Repeat Violation: Violations of this same requirement were previously found in Correction Orders that DHS issued on:

· July 25, 2024

· February 27, 2023

Corrective Action Required: Immediately and on an ongoing basis, the license holder must ensure the provision and documentation of administrative reviews of restrictive interventions meet all applicable requirements. Within 45 days receipt of this order, submit a revised form or template for the documentation of administrative reviews that demonstrates compliance.

22. Violation: One resident file reviewed for requirements governing discharge and aftercare plans (resident file numbered 5) did not meet requirements in the following ways:

a. Documentation did not demonstrate that the discharge plan was developed at least 10 days before discharge;

b. The aftercare plan did not include the following:

i. Medication, dosage, and clinical rationale;

ii. Contact information for internal PRTF providers; and

iii. Contact information for the PRTF educational provider; and

c. There was no documentation to demonstrate the license holder shared documents related to the resident’s care in their facility to any mental health providers who will be providing aftercare services.

Rule Violated: Minnesota Variance, section R2960V.07, subpart 3, items B and C.

Corrective Action Required: Immediately and on an ongoing basis, the license holder must ensure discharge documentation and aftercare plans meet all applicable requirements.

Repeat Violation: In a Correction Order that DHS issued on July 25, 2024, you were previously found in violation of this same rule.

23. Violation: Three of seven resident files reviewed for requirements governing documentation standards did not meet requirements in the following ways:

a. Documentation in the resident’s file did not include the staff person’s titles (resident files numbered 1 through 7); and

b. Daily documentation did not include observations about the resident’s symptoms (resident files numbered 1, 2, and 7).

Rule Violated: Minnesota Variance, section R2960V.07, subpart 1, item B, and R2960V.19, subparts 2 and 3.

Repeat Violation: In a Correction Order that DHS issued on July 25, 2024, you were previously found in violation of this same rule.

Corrective Action Required: Immediately and on an ongoing basis, the license holder must ensure resident file documentation meets all applicable requirements.

Written Response Required

If you fail to correct the violation(s) specified in the Correction Order within the prescribed time lines the Commissioner may issue an Order of Conditional License or may impose a fine and order other licensing sanctions pursuant to Minnesota Statutes, sections 245A.06 and 245A.07.

Submissions required as part of the corrective action ordered must be sent to your licensor by email at tina.christensen@state.mn.us or by mail:

Commissioner, Department of Human Services

ATTN: Tina Christensen

Licensing Division

PO Box 64242

St. Paul, MN 55164-0242

YOUR RIGHT TO REQUEST RECONSIDERATION

You have the right to request reconsideration of this order and the cited violations. Your request must:

· Be in writing

· List each violation you are challenging and identify what is inaccurate or incomplete about the information in the order

· Supply information that is accurate or more complete

· Be made before the deadlines provided below

If you are mailing your request, it must be received by DHS within 20 calendar days from when you received this order. If you do not meet this deadline, you lose your right to request reconsideration. The timeline to appeal began when you received this order. Please send it to:

Office of Inspector General

Legal Counsel’s Office

Attn: Licensing Legal Unit

PO Box 64953

St. Paul, MN 55164-0953

If your request is being personally delivered, it must be received by DHS within 20 calendar days from when you received this order. Please bring it to:

Commissioner, Department of Human Services

Office of Inspector General, Legal Counsel’s Office - Licensing

444 Lafayette Road North

St. Paul, MN 55155

Legal authority

This action is taken under Minnesota Statutes, section 245A.06, subdivision 1. The timeline to request reconsideration of the order is provided in Minnesota Statutes, section 245A.06, subdivision 2.

Questions

If you have any further questions regarding this matter, you may contact me at 651-431-6610 or at tina.christensen@state.mn.us .

Sincerely,

Tina Christensen, Senior Licensor

Licensing Division

Office of Inspector General

PO Box 64242 • Saint Paul, Minnesota • 55164-0242 • An Equal Opportunity and Veteran Friendly Employer
