reports.raw_content:
October 10, 2025

Michelle Murray, Authorized Agent

Nexus Family Healing

505 Highway 169 North

Plymouth, MN 55441-6447

License Number: 1108377 (CRF)

Report Numbers: 202402510, 202403620, and 202502247

CORRECTION ORDER

Dear Katie Perzel:

On June 9, 2025 through 11, 2025, the Department of Human Services (DHS) conducted a licensing review and investigation at your facility located at 2121 Campus Drive SE, Rochester, Minnesota, 55904-4733. As a result of this visit, DHS determined that you are in violation of 7 of the children’s residential facilities rules and statutes. As a result, DHS is issuing this order which requires you to take the correction action as described under each violation. Details of our findings are provided below. Our next steps and your options are also detailed.

LICENSING VIOLATIONS

DHS determined that your program did not follow licensing rules and statutes, as described below.

PRACTICES

1. Violation: One of five grievances reviewed did not meet requirements. There was no documentation that the person filing the grievance received a response within the required five day timeline.

Rule Violated: Minnesota Rules, part 2960.0080, subpart 18, item A.

Corrective Action Required: Immediately and on an ongoing basis, the license holder must ensure grievance procedures meets all applicable requirements.

PERSONNEL FILES

2. Violation: Two of two personnel files reviewed for requirements governing orientation training did not meet requirements in the following ways:

a. There was no documentation of trainings being completed prior to having unsupervised contact with residents:

i. Emergency procedures, including evacuation routes, emergency telephone numbers, severe storm and tornado procedures, and location of facility alarms and equipment (personnel file numbered 2);

ii. Cultural diversity, gender sensitivity and culturally specific services (resident file numbered 2); and

iii. Data practices regulation and issues (resident files numbered 2 and 3);

b. There was no documentation of trainings being completed within 72 hours of first providing direct contact services:

i. Vulnerable adult maltreatment reporting requirements and definitions (personnel file numbered 2)

ii. The license holders program abuse prevention plan (personnel file numbered 2); and

iii. All internal policies and procedures related to the prevention and reporting of maltreatment of individuals receiving services (personnel file numbered 2).

Statute/Rule Violated: Minnesota Statutes, section 245A.65, subdivision 3 and Minnesota Rules, part 2960.0100, subpart 3, item A.

Repeat Violation: This is a repeat violation. The license holder was cited for a similar violation in a Correction Order dated June 24, 2022.

Correction Action Required: Immediately and on an ongoing basis, the license holder must ensure that orientation training meets all applicable requirements.

RESIDENT FILES

3. Violation: One of three resident files reviewed for requirements governing resident information did not meet requirements (resident file numbered 3). The file did not contain documentation that the license holder gathered the following information about the resident:

a. The race or cultural heritage and languages the resident speaks and writes was not dated, so the DHS licensor was unable to determine if it was completed within five working days of admission;

b. The spiritual or religious affiliation of the resident and the residents family; and

c. The placing agency’s case plan goals for the resident.

Rule Violated: Minnesota Rules, part 2960.0070, subpart 3, item B.

Repeat Violation: This is a repeat violation. The license holder was cited for a similar violation in a Correction Order dated June 24, 2022.

Corrective Action Required: Immediately and on an ongoing basis, the license holder must ensure that resident information meets all applicable requirements.

4. Violation: One of three resident files reviewed for requirements governing inventory and handling of resident property did not meet requirements (resident file numbered 1). The license holder completed the inventory of the resident’s personal property, but did not have two staff sign off on the inventory after the resident did not sign.

Rule Violated: Minnesota Rules, part 2960.0070, subpart 4.

Repeat Violation: This is a repeat violation. The license holder was cited for a similar violation in a Correction Order dated June 24, 2022.

Corrective Action Required: Immediately and on an ongoing basis, the license holder must ensure that admission procedures meet all applicable requirements.

5. Violation: One resident file reviewed for requirements governing vulnerable adult orientation did not meet requirements (resident file numbered 4). There was no documentation to demonstrate that the resident was oriented to the program abuse prevention plan.

Statute Violated: Minnesota Statute, section 245A.65, subdivision 2.

Repeat Violation: This is a repeat violation. The license holder was cited for a similar violation in a Correction Order dated June 24, 2022.

Corrective Action Required: Immediately and on an ongoing basis, the license holder must ensure that vulnerable adult orientation and individual abuse prevention plans meet all applicable requirements.

6. Violation: One of three resident files reviewed for requirements governing health care services did not meet requirements (resident file numbered 1). There was no documentation that the license holder contacted a newly admitted resident’s prescribing medically licensed person to verify information regarding prescribed medication, including instructions about how the medication must be administered, the symptoms that the medication will alleviate, and the symptoms that would warrant consultation with the physician.

Rule Violated: Minnesota Rules, part 2960.0080, subpart 11, item D.

Repeat Violation: This is a repeat violation. The license holder was cited for a similar violation in a Correction Order dated June 24, 2022.

Corrective Action Required: Immediately and on an ongoing basis, the license holder must ensure that health care services meet all applicable requirements.

7. Violation: One resident file was reviewed for requirements governing a no-eject discharge (resident file numbered 1) and did not meet requirements. There was no documentation the license holder determined whether the license holder, treatment team, interested persons, if any, and the resident can develop additional strategies to resolve the issues leading to the discharge and to permit the resident an opportunity to continue to receive services from the license holder.

Rule Violated: Minnesota Rules, part 2960.0090, subpart 2.

Corrective Action Required: Immediately and on an ongoing basis, the license holder must ensure that discharge procedures meets all applicable requirements.

If you fail to correct the violations specified in the Correction Order within the prescribed timelines the Commissioner may issue an Order of Conditional License or may impose a fine and order other licensing sanctions pursuant to Minnesota Statutes, sections 245A.06 and 245A.07.

Submissions required as part of a corrective action ordered must be sent to DHS Licensing at:

1. By secure email to: maddy.moore@state.mn.us; or

2. By mail to: Commissioner, Department of Human Services

ATTN: Maddy Moore

Licensing Division

PO Box 64242

St. Paul, MN 55164-0242

YOUR RIGHT TO REQUEST RECONSIDERATION

You have the right to request reconsideration of this order and the cited violations. Your request must:

· Be in writing

· List each violation you are challenging and identify what is inaccurate or incomplete about the information in the order

· Supply information that is accurate or more complete

· Be made before the deadlines provided below

If you are mailing your request, it must be received by DHS within 20 calendar days from when you received this order. If you do not meet this deadline, you lose your right to request reconsideration. The timeline to appeal began when you received this order. Please send it to:

Commissioner, Department of Human Services

Office of Inspector General

Legal Counsel’s Office

Attn: Licensing Legal Unit

PO Box 64953

St. Paul, MN 55164-0953

If your request is being personally delivered, it must be received by DHS within 20 calendar days from when you received this order. Please bring it to:

Commissioner, Department of Human Services

Office of Inspector General

Legal Counsel’s Office

Attn: Licensing Legal Unit

444 Lafayette Road North

St. Paul, MN 55155

Questions

If you have any further questions regarding this matter, you may contact Maddy Moore, Senior Licensor, at 651-431-4585.

Sincerely,

Maddy Moore, Senior Licensor
Licensing Division

reports.raw_content:
October 9, 2024

Michelle Murray, Authorized Agent

Nexus Family Healing

505 Highway 169 N. STE 500

Plymouth, MN 55441

License Number:   1108377

Dear Michelle Murray:

On August 22, 2024, the Minnesota Department of Human Services (DHS), Division of Licensing, received your request for reconsideration of the citation in the Correction Order (Attachment A) issued to you by the Department of Human Services on August 8, 2024. You requested reconsideration only of violations b and c of the citation. Violations a and d of the citation are therefore final and not within the scope of this review.

Citation 1: Violation: The license holder did not meet critical incident reporting requirements. The following incidents met the definition of a critical incident and were not submitted to DHS within 10 days of learning of the incident:

a. An incident dated July 29, 2023, was submitted on June 2, 2024;

b. An incident dated September 1, 2023, was submitted on June 3, 2024;

c. An incident dated May 26, 2024, was submitted on June 18, 2024; and

d. Two incidents dated July 6, 2024, were submitted on July 27, 2024.

Request for Reconsideration. You submitted a letter requesting reconsideration in which you indicate the program was in compliance with the applicable law at the time the citation was issued. With regard to item b, you indicate the program reported the incident on September 26, 2023, and the report sent on June 3, 2024, was a duplicate of the same incident. With regard to item c, you indicate that although you reported the incident to the Office of Ombudsman on June 3, 2024, you believe the incident did not require a critical incident report to DHS because the injury did not require hospitalization or serious medical treatment. You acknowledge that the incident involved a head injury that required medical attention. You submitted supporting documentation.

Reconsideration Determination. Under Minnesota Rules, part 2960.0080, subpart 17, item A, the license holder must report critical incidents and the maltreatment of a resident according to items A to D. Item A requires that the license holder report critical incidents of a serious nature that involve or endanger the life or safety of the resident or others to the commissioner of human services within ten days of the day of the occurrence on forms approved by the commissioner of human services or corrections. The license holder must maintain records of all critical incidents on file in the facility.

Under Minnesota Rules, part 2960.0020, subpart 24, “critical incident” means an occurrence which involves a resident and requires the program to make a response that is not part of the program’s ordinary daily routine. Examples of critical incidents include, but are not limited to, suicide, attempted suicide, homicide, death of a resident, injury that is either life-threatening or requires medical treatment, fire which requires fire department response, alleged maltreatment of a resident, assault of a resident, assault by a resident, client-to-client sexual contact, or other act or situation which would require a response by law enforcement, the fire department, an ambulance, or another emergency response provider.

With regard to violation b, there is no record of the report being submitted until June 3, 2024, and no documentation of an earlier report was provided. Even if the report had been submitted on September 26, 2023, it would not have met the requirements of the rule. The incident occurred on September 1, 2023, which would have been twenty-five days prior to the date of the report, and the rule requires that critical incidents be reported within ten days of the occurrence.

With regard to violation c, you acknowledge the incident involved an injury that required medical attention, and the definition of “critical incident” includes, but is not limited to, an injury that “requires medical treatment.” The definition of “critical incident” means an occurrence which requires the program to make a response that is not part of the program’s ordinary daily routine. According to the Critical Incident Reporting Form you submitted on June 18, 2024, the individual involved in the incident sustained a head injury and was assessed via a telehealth visit with an emergency room physician. Also, an email from the program to DHS dated June 18, 2024, indicated the program would follow up with a critical incident report.

There is therefore sufficient evidence to support the violations and they are affirmed, with the modification that the correct authority for the citation is Minnesota Rules, part 2960.0080, item A, and Minnesota Rules, part 2960.0020, subpart 24. The correction order incorrectly cited Minnesota Statutes, section 245I.13 as the authority for the citation.

Disposition: The Commissioner has reviewed the request for reconsideration and all of the information submitted in response to the correction order. The Commissioner finds there is sufficient evidence to support the citation, and it is affirmed. This is a final agency decision.

Sincerely,

Frances Simon Standing, Attorney

Legal Counsel’s Office

Office of Inspector General

PO Box 64242 • Saint Paul, Minnesota • 55164-0242 • An Equal Opportunity and Veteran Friendly Employer

reports.raw_content:
June 24, 2022

Michelle Murray, Authorized Agent

Nexus Family Healing

505 Highway 169 N., Suite 500

Plymouth, MN 55441

License Number: 1108377

CORRECTION ORDER

Dear Michelle Murray:

On April 26 through 29, 2022, a licensing review of Southeast Regional Crisis Center Nexus located at 2121 Campus Dr. SE., Rochester, MN 55904, was conducted to determine compliance with state and federal laws and rules governing the provision of children's residential facilities under Minnesota Rules, parts 2960.0010 through 2960.0120 and residential shelter services under Minnesota Rules, parts 2960.0510 through 2960.0530 (CRF). As a result of this licensing review a Correction Order is being issued.

A. Reason for Correction Order

Pursuant to Minnesota Statutes, section 245A.06, if the Commissioner of the Department of Human Services (DHS) finds that an applicant or license holder has failed to comply with an applicable law or rule and this failure does not imminently endanger the health, safety, or rights of the persons served by the program, the Commissioner may issue a Correction Order to the applicant or license holder.

The following violations of state or federal laws and rules were determined as a result of the licensing review. Corrective action for each violation is required by Minnesota Statutes, section 245A.06 and is hereby ordered by the Commissioner of Human Services.

Policies and Practices

1. Citation: Minnesota Rules, part 2960.0080, subpart 4, items B and D.

Violation: The facility rules and due process system for residents did not meet requirements for the following topics:

a. The consequences that will be applied in recognizing and rewarding acceptable behavior and modifying unacceptable behavior; and

b. The due process system that governs the facility's use of disciplinary consequences.

Corrective Action Ordered: Immediately and on an ongoing basis, the license holder must ensure the facility rules and due process system meets all applicable requirements. Within 30 days of receipt of this order, submit a revised policy that meets all applicable requirements.

2. Citation: Minnesota Statues, section 245A.04, subdivision 14, paragraph (b).

Violation: The license holder failed to monitor the implementation of practices and policies. The license holders practice was not consistent with the following policies as described below:

a. The license holder reported that the Nursing Supervisor is responsible for providing consultation and review of the administration of medications in which they review at least every patient’s record at least once a month. The license holder’s NURS-708 policy indicates that the Nursing Supervisor will review the Electronic Medication Administration Record (EMAR) weekly for review and consultation; therefore, the license holder failed to implement the weekly reviews according to their policy.

b. The license holder reported that it is their practice and policy to obtain a written signature from a parent or guardian on designated forms to document permission to administer medications to the resident. The NURS-706 policy and work flow chart indicates that the permission is captured on the Client Medication List Upon Admission and the Medical Consent forms. The Medical Consent form was incomplete.

Corrective Action Ordered: Immediately and on an ongoing basis, the license holder must ensure the monitoring of the implementation of polices meets all applicable requirements.

Within 30 days of receipt of this order, review and submit policy NURS-708 and NURS-706 that describes the current practice for the program.

Personnel Files

3. Citation: Minnesota Statutes, section 245A.65, subdivision 3 and Minnesota Rules, part 2960.0100, subpart 3, item A.

Violation: Ten of ten personnel files reviewed for requirements governing orientation training (personnel files numbered 1 through 10) did not meet requirements. Staff were not oriented to the license holder’s program abuse prevention plan within 72 hours of first providing direct contact services.

Corrective Action Ordered: Immediately and on an ongoing basis, the license holder must ensure the provision and documentation of training meets all applicable requirements. Within 30 days of receipt of this order, submit documentation that staff persons identified above were trained on the program abuse prevention plan to demonstrate compliance.

Resident Files

4. Citation: Minnesota Rules, part 2960.0070, subpart 3, item B.

Violation: One of three resident files reviewed for requirements governing resident information (resident file numbered 3) did not meet requirements. The resident’s file did not contain the placing agency’s case plan goals nor did the license holder document and place in the file the attempts made to obtain the placing agency’s case plan goals for the resident.

Corrective Action Ordered: Immediately and on an ongoing basis, the license holder must ensure resident admission information meets all applicable requirements.

5. Citation: Minnesota Rules, parts 2960.0050, subpart 3, item A, 2960.0080, subpart 4, 2960.0520, subparts 2, items A and B, and 3, item B and C.

Violation: Three of three resident files reviewed for requirements governing admission procedures and plans for immediate needs did not meet requirements in the following ways:

a. The facility rules and due process for residents was not explained verbally and provided in writing to the resident (resident files numbered 1 through 3);

b. There was no documentation to demonstrate that within 24 hours of admission, a written copy of the resident’s basic rights was explained verbally and provided to the resident (resident files numbered 1 and 3); and

c. There was no documentation to demonstrate that when a need for examination was determined from the basic health screening, the license holder notified the resident’s case manager of the need to make an appointment with a medically licensed person to complete the required examination (resident file numbered 2);

d. The license holder did not develop a plan to reduce the resident’s risk of maltreatment while in the program in accordance to their assessment of vulnerability to maltreatment (resident files numbered 1 and 3);

e. The short-term objectives and methods for meeting the identified needs were not specified in the plan for immediate needs (resident files numbered 1 through 3); and

f. The license holder did not indicate their responsibilities for meeting the resident’s needs identified in the plan for immediate needs (resident files numbered 1 through 3).

Corrective Action Ordered: Immediately and on an ongoing basis, the license holder must ensure admission procedures and plans for immediate needs meet all applicable requirements. Within 30 days receipt of this order, submit a plan to reduce the resident’s risk for maltreatment and a plan for immediate needs for one resident to demonstrate compliance.

6. Citation: Minnesota Rules, parts 2960.0070, subpart 4 and 2960.0090, subpart 3.

Violation: Three of three resident files reviewed for requirements governing the inventory and handling of resident property did not meet requirements in the following ways:

a. The file did not contain a personal property inventory form upon admission that was signed by the resident (resident files numbered 1 and 3); and

b. The file did not include a signed receipt demonstrating the license holder returned all of the residents’ personal property (resident files numbered 1 through 3).

Corrective Action Ordered: Immediately and on an ongoing basis, the license holder must ensure the inventory and handling of resident property meets all applicable requirements.

7. Citation: Minnesota Statutes, section 245A.65, subdivisions 1, paragraph (c); 2, paragraphs (a) and (b); and 626.557, subdivision 14, paragraph (c).

Violation: One resident file was reviewed for requirements governing maltreatment of vulnerable adults (resident file numbered 2) and did not meet requirements in the following ways:

a. Documentation to demonstrate the resident was oriented within 24 hours of admission to the program, or 72 hours for persons who would benefit more from a later orientation, to the internal and external reporting procedures governing maltreatment of vulnerable adults, including the telephone number for MAARC and the program abuse prevention plan; and

b. The Individual Abuse Prevention Plan (IAPP) did not contain the following:

1) An individualized assessment of the person’s susceptibility to abuse by other individuals, including other vulnerable adults;

2) Specific measures to be taken to minimize the risk of abuse to that person when the individual assessment indicates the need for measures.

Corrective Action Ordered: Immediately and on an ongoing basis, the license holder must ensure the provision of maltreatment of vulnerable adults meets all applicable requirements.

8. Citation: Minnesota Rules, part 2960.0080, subpart 11, item D.

Violation: Three of three resident files reviewed for requirements governing safe storage and delivery of medications (resident files numbered 1 through 3) did not meet requirements in the following ways:

a. There was no documentation to demonstrate the license holder contacted the resident’s prescribing medically licensed person to verify the following information regarding their prescription medication:

1) The symptoms that he medication will alleviate; and

2) The symptoms that would warrant consultation with the physician.

Corrective Action Ordered: Immediately and on an ongoing basis, the license holder must ensure the provision and documentation of health care meets all applicable requirements. Within 30 days receipt of this order, submit medication verification documentation for two residents to demonstrate compliance.

9. Citation: Minnesota Rules, part 2960.0090, subpart 2.

Violation: One resident file was reviewed for requirements governing a no-eject discharge (resident file numbered 3) and did not meet requirements. The file did not contain documentation of the following:

a. The license holder conferred with other interested persons to review the issues involved in the decision to discharge a resident prior to reaching their case plan goals;

b. If any additional strategies to resolve the issues leading to discharge could have been developed to allow the resident opportunity to continue receiving services; and

c. The reasons for discharge and the alternatives considered or attempted when the decision to discharge were warranted.

Corrective Action Ordered: Immediately and on an ongoing basis, the license holder must ensure the documentation of no-eject discharges meets all applicable requirements.

B. Recommendations

The following recommendations are not requirements of Minnesota Rules or laws governing your services or facility. These recommendations are provided to call your attention to areas where your facility is in minimum compliance with the requirements of rules or laws, but it would be advisable to strengthen your efforts in these areas.

1. During the licensing review it was unclear if program staff were utilizing time out procedures. DHS Licensing recommends the license holder incorporate the definition of time out in its emergency procedure and explicitly indicate if it is permissible or not. If the license holder chooses to utilize time out the license holder must follow the required requirements under MN Rule 2960.0080, subpart 5, item D.

2. DHS licensing recommends the license holder to coordinate with similar/alike licenses across the organization to ensure a better understanding and compliance on all applicable rules and statutes with Minnesota Rules, chapter 2960.

If you fail to correct the violations specified in the Correction Order within the prescribed time lines the Commissioner may issue an Order of Conditional License or may impose a fine and order other licensing sanctions pursuant to Minnesota Statutes, sections 245A.06 and 245A.07.

Submissions required as part of a corrective action ordered must be sent to DHS Licensing at:

1. By secure email to: tina.christensen@state.mn.us; or

2. By mail to:

Commissioner, Department of Human Services

ATTN: Tina Christensen

Licensing Division

PO Box 64242

St. Paul, MN 55164-0242

C. Right to Request Reconsideration

If you believe any of the citations are in error, you have the right to request that the Commissioner of Human Services reconsider the parts of the Correction Order that you believe to be in error. The request for reconsideration must be in writing and received by the Commissioner within 20 calendar days after receipt of this report. Your request for reconsideration must be sent to:

Commissioner, Department of Human Services

ATTN: Legal Unit

Licensing Division

PO Box 64242

St. Paul, MN 55164-0242

Please note that a request for reconsideration does not stay any provisions or requirements of the Correction Order. The Commissioner’s disposition of a request for reconsideration is final and not subject to appeal under Minnesota Statutes, chapter 14.

If you have any questions regarding this Correction Order, please contact me as soon as possible.

Tina Christensen, Human Services Senior Licensor

Licensing Division

Office of Inspector General

651-431-6610

PO Box 64242 • Saint Paul, Minnesota • 55164-0242 • An Equal Opportunity and Veteran Friendly Employer

February 25, 2026                   CERTIFIED MAIL

Andrea Drake, Authorized Agent

Rising Hope Foundation

16966 County Road 2

Brainerd, MN 56401

License Number 1116745 (CRF)

ORDER TO PAY A FINE

Dear Andrea:

The Department of Human Services (DHS) is ordering you to pay a fine of $100 for one licensing violation at your Children’s Residential Facility (CRF) located at 16966 County Road 2 in Brainerd, MN 56401. Details of our findings are provided below. Our next steps and your options are also detailed.

REASON FOR FINE

1.  Other Licensing Violation

The Department of Human Services (DHS) and your program, The Freedom Ranch, located at 16966 County Road 2 Brainerd, MN, 56401, entered into a settlement agreement on April 25, 2025. Term number 4, paragraph (c) of the settlement agreement requires the license holder to submit findings related to their self-monitoring of compliance to their licensor on the 15th of the month following the quarter.

As of January 15, 2026, DHS had not received documentation of the required self-monitoring of compliance regarding 2025 Quarter 4.

Fine: $100

Corrective Action Required: Maintain compliance with all terms of the settlement agreement. Under term number 6 of the settlement agreement dated April 25, 2025, the license holder’s failure to comply with the terms of the agreement may result in immediate reinstatement of the Conditional Order.

Statute Violated: Minnesota Statutes, section 245A.06, subdivision 1.

Billing and Payment of the Fine

DHS will send you an invoice for the $100 fine. Payment must be made as directed on the invoice.

If you request a contested case hearing, as described below, do not pay the fine at this time. Following the contested case hearing, the Commissioner of DHS will issue a final order on this matter.

Please note, you may not avoid payment of this fine by closing, selling, or otherwise transferring the license to a third party. If this occurs, you are personally responsible for payment. If you do not pay the fine on or before the date specified on the invoice and you did not request a contested case hearing, as described below, the Commissioner may issue a second fine, may not issue or reissue a license, or may suspend the license until the license holder pays the fine.

YOUR RIGHT TO APPEAL

You have the right to appeal the fine. Your request must be in writing and clearly state that you are requesting a contested case hearing for this matter. Your request must be made before the deadlines provided below. If you do not meet this deadline, you lose your right to an administrative appeal. The timeline to appeal began when you received this order.

If you are mailing your request, it must be sent by certified mail and postmarked within 10 calendar days from when you receive this order. Please send it to:

Commissioner, Department of Human Services

Office of Inspector General

Legal Counsel’s Office

Attention: Licensing Legal Unit

PO Box 64953

St. Paul, MN 55164-0953

If your request is being personally delivered, it must be received by DHS within 10 calendar days from when you receive this order. Please bring it to:

Commissioner, Department of Human Services

Office of Inspector General

Legal Counsel’s Office

Attention: Licensing Legal Unit

444 Lafayette Road North

St. Paul, MN 55155

Upon DHS’ receipt of your timely appeal, your case would be scheduled for a contested case hearing in front of an Administrative Law Judge. Following this hearing, the Commissioner of DHS will issue a final order.

Legal representation at the contested case hearing

You do not need a lawyer to appeal. However, a lawyer can help you with your appeal. The state or county will not get you a lawyer and will not pay for a lawyer. If you cannot afford a lawyer, you may be able to get free legal advice or help with your appeal. To find out if free help is available, contact: Volunteer Lawyers Network at 612-752-6677; Central Minnesota Legal Services at 612-332-8151; Southern Minnesota Legal Services at 651-222-4731; or go to www.lawhelpmn.org to find a local legal services program that may be able to help you.

You can also find information on contested cases from the Office of Administrative Hearings website at https://mn.gov/oah/self-help. Click on Administrative Law Overview, then click on Administrative Law Contested Case Hearing Guide for a list of frequently asked questions.

Legal authority for this licensing action

· This action is taken under Minnesota Statutes, section 245A.07, subdivision 1, paragraph (a), which describes under which conditions DHS may impose a fine against a license.

· DHS may impose a $100 fine for each occurrence of a licensing violation that is not related to health, safety, supervision, or maltreatment under Minnesota Statutes, section 245A.07, subdivision 3, paragraph (c)(4)(v).

· Minnesota Statutes, section 245A.07, subdivision 3, paragraph (c)(5) states that license holders may not avoid payment of a fine by closing, selling, or transferring a license.

· Minnesota Statutes, section 245A.04, subdivision 7, paragraph (d)(4), states that DHS shall not issue or reissue a license if the applicant, license holder, or controlling individual has an outstanding debt related to a license fee, licensing fine, or settlement agreement for which payment is delinquent.

· Minnesota Statutes, section 245A.07, subdivision 3, paragraph (c)(2), states that that the license holder shall pay the fine assessed on or before the payment date specified, and if the license holder does not do so the commissioner may issue a second fine or suspend the license until the license holder complies.

· License holders have a right to appeal licensing actions and request a contested case hearing, under Minnesota Statutes, chapter 14 and Minnesota Rules, parts 1400.8505 to 1400.8612.

Questions

If you have any further questions regarding this matter, you may contact Paula Halverson, Manager, at 651-431-5653.

Sincerely,

Paula Halverson, Unit Manager

Licensing Division

Office of Inspector General

PO Box 64242 • Saint Paul, Minnesota • 55164-0242 • An Equal Opportunity and Veteran Friendly Employer

October 3, 2025

Andrea Drake, Authorized Agent

Rising Hope Foundation

16966 County Road 2

Brainerd, MN 56401

License Number: 1116745

CORRECTION ORDER

Dear Andrea Drake:

On April 25, 2025, the Department of Human Services (DHS) and your program, The Freedom Ranch, located at 16966 County Road 2 Brainerd, MN, 56401, entered into a settlement agreement. A correction order is being issued due to The Freedom Ranch not following terms of a settlement agreement as required under Minnesota Statutes, chapter 245A.

REASON FOR CORRECTION ORDER

Pursuant to Minnesota Statutes, section 245A.06, if the Commissioner of the Department of Human Services (DHS) finds that an applicant or license holder has failed to comply with an applicable law or rule and this failure does not imminently endanger the health, safety, or rights of the persons served by the program, the Commissioner may issue a Correction Order to the applicant or license holder.

The following violations of state or federal laws and rules were determined as a result of not complying with terms of the settlement agreement. Corrective action for each violation is required by Minnesota Statutes, section 245A.06 and is hereby ordered by the Commissioner of Human Services.

VIOLATIONS

1. Violation: The license holder did not comply with term G of the settlement agreement, effective on April 25, 2025:

a. The license holder did not report to DHS a change in the role of Compliance Officer.

Statute Violated: Minnesota Statutes, section 245A.081.

Corrective Action Required: Immediately and on an ongoing basis, the license holder must ensure that all the terms of the settlement agreement are followed. Within 14 days receipt of this order, the license holder must submit to DHS Licensing a resume for any proposed Compliance Officer the identifies two years of professional experience in a regulatory or compliance position or in providing direct care services to youth in a children’s residential facility, and the ability to understand and interpret rules and statutes. The program’s Compliance Office may only allocate 50 percent of their working hours to direct care services. The program’s Compliance Officer must be approved by the program’s DHS Licensor.

Written Response Required

If you fail to correct the violation(s) specified in the Correction Order within the prescribed time lines the Commissioner may issue an Order of Conditional License or may impose a fine and order other licensing sanctions pursuant to Minnesota Statutes, sections 245A.06 and 245A.07.

Submissions required as part of the corrective action ordered must be sent to your licensor by email at hannah.horsch@state.mn.us or by mail:

Commissioner, Department of Human Services

ATTN: Hannah Horsch

Licensing Division

PO Box 64242

St. Paul, MN 55164-0242

YOUR RIGHT TO REQUEST RECONSIDERATION

You have the right to request reconsideration of this order and the cited violations. Your request must:

· Be in writing

· List each violation you are challenging and identify what is inaccurate or incomplete about the information in the order

· Supply information that is accurate or more complete

· Be made before the deadlines provided below

If you are mailing your request, it must be received by DHS within 20 calendar days from when you received this order. If you do not meet this deadline, you lose your right to request reconsideration. The timeline to appeal began when you received this order. Please send it to:

Office of Inspector General

Legal Counsel’s Office

Attn: Licensing Legal Unit

PO Box 64953

St. Paul, MN 55164-0953

If your request is being personally delivered, it must be received by DHS within 20 calendar days from when you received this order. Please bring it to:

Commissioner, Department of Human Services

Office of Inspector General, Legal Counsel’s Office - Licensing

444 Lafayette Road North

St. Paul, MN 55155

Legal authority

This action is taken under Minnesota Statutes, section 245A.06, subdivision 1. The timeline to request reconsideration of the order is provided in Minnesota Statutes, section 245A.06, subdivision 2.

Questions

If you have any further questions regarding this matter, you may contact me at 651-431-6270 or at hannah.horsch@state.mn.us.

Sincerely,

Hannah Horsch, Licensor

Licensing Division

Office of Inspector General

reports.raw_content:
August 20, 2025

Andrea Drake, Authorized Agent

Rising Hope Foundation

16966 County Road 2

Brainerd, MN 56401

License Number: 1116745

CORRECTION ORDER

Dear Andrea Drake:

On April 25, 2025, the Department of Human Services (DHS) and your program, The Freedom Ranch, located at 16966 County Road 2 Brainerd, MN, 56401, entered into a settlement agreement. A correction order is being issued due to The Freedom Ranch not following terms of a settlement agreement as required under Minnesota Statutes, chapter 245A.

REASON FOR CORRECTION ORDER

Pursuant to Minnesota Statutes, section 245A.06, if the Commissioner of the Department of Human Services (DHS) finds that an applicant or license holder has failed to comply with an applicable law or rule and this failure does not imminently endanger the health, safety, or rights of the persons served by the program, the Commissioner may issue a Correction Order to the applicant or license holder.

The following violations of state or federal laws and rules were determined as a result of not complying with terms of the settlement agreement. Corrective action for each violation is required by Minnesota Statutes, section 245A.06 and is hereby ordered by the Commissioner of Human Services.

Violations

1. Violation: The license holder did not comply with terms C and E of the settlement agreement, effective on April 25, 2025:

a. The license holder did not implement the DHS self-monitoring plan and submit a quarterly report by July 15, 2025, that included:

i. A summary of any patterns of problems identified in monthly reviews of policies and procedures, practices, and client files;

ii. A description of the strategies put in place to address any non-compliance determined;

iii. Corrective actions taken; and

iv. A determination of whether the corrective action taken was effective.

b. The license holder did not submit to DHS a personnel training plan that outlines the contents of each topic that staff are trained on, who provides the training, how the training is delivered, and how staff competency is evaluated. The training plan submitted does not include all training requirements required by Minnesota Statutes, section 245A.65, subdivision 3, and Minnesota Rules, parts 2960.0100, subparts 3 and 5, and 2960.0150, subpart 4.

Statute Violated: Minnesota Statutes, section 245A.06, subdivision 1.

Corrective Action Required: Immediately and on an ongoing basis, the license holder must ensure that all the terms of the settlement agreement are followed. Within 10 days of receipt of this order, submit a quarterly report and personnel training plan that meets all requirements of the settlement agreement and applicable statute and rule requirements. You must include a copy of the checklists used.

Written Response Required

If you fail to correct the violation(s) specified in the Correction Order within the prescribed time lines the Commissioner may issue an Order of Conditional License or may impose a fine and order other licensing sanctions pursuant to Minnesota Statutes, sections 245A.06 and 245A.07.

Submissions required as part of the corrective action ordered must be sent to your licensor by email at hannah.horsch@state.mn.us or by mail:

Commissioner, Department of Human Services

ATTN: Hannah Horsch

Licensing Division

PO Box 64242

St. Paul, MN 55164-0242

YOUR RIGHT TO REQUEST RECONSIDERATION

You have the right to request reconsideration of this order and the cited violations. Your request must:

· Be in writing

· List each violation you are challenging and identify what is inaccurate or incomplete about the information in the order

· Supply information that is accurate or more complete

· Be made before the deadlines provided below

If you are mailing your request, it must be received by DHS within 20 calendar days from when you received this order. If you do not meet this deadline, you lose your right to request reconsideration. The timeline to appeal began when you received this order. Please send it to:

Office of Inspector General

Legal Counsel’s Office

Attn: Licensing Legal Unit

PO Box 64953

St. Paul, MN 55164-0953

If your request is being personally delivered, it must be received by DHS within 20 calendar days from when you received this order. Please bring it to:

Commissioner, Department of Human Services

Office of Inspector General, Legal Counsel’s Office - Licensing

444 Lafayette Road North

St. Paul, MN 55155

Legal authority

This action is taken under Minnesota Statutes, section 245A.06, subdivision 1. The timeline to request reconsideration of the order is provided in Minnesota Statutes, section 245A.06, subdivision 2.

Questions

If you have any further questions regarding this matter, you may contact me at 651-431-6270 or at hannah.horsch@state.mn.us.

Sincerely,

Hannah Horsch, Licensor

Licensing Division

Office of Inspector General

PO Box 64242 • Saint Paul, Minnesota • 55164-0242 • An Equal Opportunity and Veteran Friendly Employer

reports.raw_content:
December 31, 2024

CERTIFIED MAIL

Jenny Cushing, Authorized Agent

The Freedom Ranch

16966 County Road 2

Brainerd, MN 56401

License Number: 1116745 (CRF)

ORDER OF CONDITIONAL LICENSE

Dear Jenny Cushing:

The Department of Human Services (DHS) is placing your license to provide at The Freedom Ranch, 16966 County Road 2 Brainerd, MN, on conditional status for two years, beginning December 31, 2024. This means you must meet certain conditions to maintain your license, detailed below. This order is based on your noncompliance with Children’s Residential Facilities licensing requirements. Details of our findings are also provided below. Our next steps and your options are also detailed.

REASON FOR THE CONDITIONAL LICENSE

On November 4 and 5, 2024, DHS licensors conducted a licensing review and investigation at your facility located at 16966 County Road 2 in Brainerd, MN. As a result of this licensing visit, the DHS licensors determined that your program failed to comply with the laws and rules that apply to licensed Children’s Residential Facilities, citing 17 violations. DHS has considered the nature and severity of these violations, as well as the health, safety, and rights of children by the program.

· Nature / severity:

o The license holder failed to maintain awake staffing and supervision on October 6, 8 and 9, 2024, and was cited in a fine order issued on October 23, 2024;

o The license holder failed to comply with background study requirements prior to staff working in a children’s residential facility (violation number 1);

o The license holder failed to comply with the terms of a background study variance (violation number 2);

o The license holder failed to retain and store program administration and client records (violation number 3);

o The license holder failed to train staff on medication administration prior to them administering medications (violation number 4);

o The license holder failed to comply with the applicable health codes and did not have a Certified Food Manager on staff (violation number 5); and

o The license holder failed to comply with requirements of staff orientation training (violation number 6).

Due to the serious nature of these violations, and the conditions in the program, which impact the health and safety of children served in your care, your license to provide group residential services in a Children’s Residential Facility is placed on a conditional status.

Licensing Violations

DHS determined that your program failed to follow licensing rules and statutes, as described below.

1. Violation: The license holder failed to meet background study requirements for two staff members required to have a background study before the individual began working in the facility (personnel files numbered 4 and 5).

As the result of a licensing review on November 4 and 5, 2024, a DHS licensor determined that the license holder did not initiate a background study on two staff persons (personnel files numbered 4 and 5) before s/he began working in the facility. Working in a children’s residential facility without having received a required notice is prohibited.

Staff (personnel files numbered 4 and 5) were hired as a Registered Nurse (RN) to provide oversight of the program’s health care and medication administration policies and procedures. The license holder did not maintain personnel files or any related background study documentation that could demonstrate when they began working in the facility. Program staff confirmed that staff (personnel files numbered 4 and 5) had been on site at the facility and had met with residents. At the time of the licensing review on November 4 and 5, 2024, the license holder had not submitted a background study request to DHS for staff (personnel files numbered 4 and 5) and as a result, had not received the notice required to allow them to work in the facility.

Rule Violated: Minnesota Statutes, section 245C.03, subdivision 1.

2. Violation: The license holder failed to comply with the terms of a background study variance issued to a staff person (personnel file numbered 6). The license holder did not ensure that the staff person always worked with another employee with a cleared background study and did not have documentation to demonstrate that the staff person received supervision for the months of April, August, or September 2024.

Rule Violated: Minnesota Statutes, section 245C.30, subdivision 1.

3. Violation: The license holder failed to retain and store records in a manner that allowed for review by the commissioner during the licensing review and licensing investigations; therefore, the following records were not maintained in accordance with applicable state or federal law, regulation, or rule for the following:

a. Program administration records of satisfaction surveys according to Minnesota Rules, part 2960.0140, subpart 1, item B; and

b. Client file paperwork, including verification of service delivery according to Minnesota Rules, part 2960.0180, subpart 3, item C (resident file numbered 1 and 2).

During the licensing review, a staff member informed licensors that the documents had either been thrown out or were otherwise misplaced.

Statute Violated: Minnesota Statutes, section 245A.041, subdivision 3.

4. Violation: Eleven of fifteen personnel files reviewed for requirements of rules governing medication assistance training did not meet requirements. Medication administration records demonstrated that the staff persons administered medications to residents without having received training by a registered nurse during the months of January through October 2024 (personnel files numbered 3, and 6 through 15).

Rule Violated: Minnesota Rules, part 2960.0080, subpart 11, item D.

5. Violation: The license holder failed to comply with the applicable health codes. The license holder did not have a Certified Food Manager on staff from October 14, 2024, to November 5, 2024.

Rule Violated: Minnesota Rules, part 2960.0120, subpart 2.

6. Violation: Six of six personnel files reviewed for orientation training did not meet requirements:

a. There was no documentation that staff successfully completed orientation training before having unsupervised contact with residents on the following topics (personnel files numbered 1, 4, and 5):

i. Emergency procedures;

ii. Maltreatment of minor reporting requirements under Minnesota Statutes, section 260E;

iii. Cultural diversity and gender sensitivity, culturally specific services, and information about discrimination and racial bias issues;

iv. General and special needs, including disability needs, of residents and families served;

v. Operational policies and procedures; and

vi. Data practice regulations and issues.

b. There was no documentation that staff were oriented to the following required topics:

i. Gender issues, including the psychosocial development of boys and girls (personnel files numbered 1, 4, and 5);

ii. Sexual orientation issues (personnel files numbered 1, 4, and 5);

iii. Drug and alcohol policy (personnel files numbered 1, 2, 4, and 5); and

iv. Staff’s duties in implementing the program’s policies and procedures (personnel files numbered 1 through 6).

c. The license holder did not have documentation of the date and number of hours of orientation completed in each topic area and the name of the entity who provided the training (personnel files numbered 1 through 6).

Statute and Rule Violated: Minnesota Statutes, section 245A.04, subdivisions 1, paragraph (c) and 14, paragraph (b), and Minnesota Rules, parts 2960.0100, subparts 3, item A, and 5, and 2960.0150, subpart 4, item C.

7. Violation: Two of two resident files reviewed for rules governing no-eject procedures did not meet requirements (resident files numbered 1 and 2). For residents who were discharged prior to reaching their case plan goals, requirements were not met in the following ways:

a. There was no documentation to demonstrate the license holder conferred with other interested persons to review the issues involved in the decision;

b. There was no documentation to demonstrate the license holder determined whether the license holder, treatment team, interested persons, and the resident could develop any additional strategies to resolve the issues leading to discharge and to permit the resident an opportunity to continue to receive services from the license holder; and

c. Documentation did not identify alternatives to discharge that were considered or attempted.

Rule Violated: Minnesota Rules, part 2960.0090, subpart 2.

8. Violation: Three of three resident files reviewed for rules governing admission procedures did not meet requirements in the following ways:

a. The inventory of the resident’s personal property did not include the resident’s signature, or two staff signatures if the resident refused to sign (resident files numbered 1);

b. There was not documentation that the license holder communicated to the resident the facility’s rules and due process system (resident files numbered 1 and 2);

c. There was no documentation that a written copy of the resident’s basic rights was given to the resident and explained to them if the resident was incapable of understanding the written document within 24 hours of admission (resident files numbered 1 and 2);

d. There was not documentation that the residents’ guardians were informed that information on the resident’s basic rights is available (resident files numbered 1 through 3);

e. There was not documentation that copies of the facility rules were not made available to all residents at the time of admission (resident files numbered 1 and 2); and

f. There was not documentation that residents were provided with a copy of a description of the applicable programs and activities available to residents in the facility within 24 hours of admission (resident files numbered 1 through 3).

Rule Violated: Minnesota Rules, parts 2960.0050, subpart 3, items A and B, 2960.0070, subpart 4, 2960.0080, subpart 4, and 2960.0160, subpart 4, items A and B.

9. Violation: The license holder failed to monitor the implementation of their policies and procedures. The license holder had two different versions of information on resident rights.

Statute Violated: Minnesota Statutes, section 245A.04, subdivision 14, paragraph (b).

10. Violation: The license holder did not have a copy of the rights posted in the staff work station.

Rule Violated: Minnesota Rules, part 2960.0050, subpart 3, item D.

11. Violation: The license holder’s grievance procedure did not include the following:

a. The allowance of a resident’s parent or legal representative, a guardian, or a concerned person in the resident’s life to make a formal complaint or suggestion or express a concern about any aspect of the resident’s care during the resident’s stay in the facility;

b. Who the authorized person to resolve the complaint is and the ability to carry the grievance forward to the highest level of administration;

c. That a person who reports a grievance will not be subject to adverse action by the license holder as a result or filing the grievance; and

d. That the person filing a grievance will receive a response within five days.

Rule Violated: Minnesota Rules, part 2960.0080, subpart 18, item A.

12. Violation: Three of three resident files reviewed for rules governing admission criteria did not meet requirements. Documentation did not include the following required components:

a. Whether the program could meet the resident’s needs (resident files numbered 1 through 3),

b. Whether the resident was a danger to themselves or others (resident file numbered 1); and

c. Whether the program was able to meet the resident’s cultural, emotional, educational, mental health, or physical needs (resident files numbered 1 through 3).

Rule Violated: Minnesota Rules, part 2960.0160, subpart 2, items A and C.

13. Violation: Three of three resident files reviewed for rules governing admission screenings did not meet requirements in the following ways:

a. The health screening was not completed (resident files numbered 1 through 3);

b. A mental health screening was not completed (resident file numbered 1);

c. A substance use disorder screening was not completed (resident files numbered 1 and 2);

d. An education screening was not completed (resident files numbered 1 through 3);

e. A gender-specific screening was not completed (resident files numbered 1 through 3); and

f. Inquiries and the results of the inquiries regarding the degree to which the resident and the resident’s family desires for the resident’s family to be involved during the resident’s stay at the facility were not documented (resident files numbered 1 through 3).

Rule Violated: Minnesota Rules, part 2960.0070, subpart 5, items A, B, C, and D.

14. Violation: Three of three resident files reviewed for rules governing treatment plans did not meet requirements in the following ways:

a. There was no documentation to demonstrate that they began to develop a treatment plan within ten days of admission (resident file numbered 1);

b. There was no documentation to demonstrate the resident was assigned to a designated staff person to monitor and assist the resident in implementing the treatment plan (resident files numbered 1 through 3).

Rule Violated: Minnesota Rules, parts 2960.0180, subpart 2, item B

15. Violation: Three of three resident files reviewed for rules governing health care did not meet requirements. The license holder did not have documentation of the following:

a. That the license holder contacted the resident’s prescribing medically licensed person to verify the symptoms that the medication will alleviate (resident files numbered 1 and 2) and the symptoms that would warrant consultation with the physician (resident files numbered 1 through 3);

b. Attempts to contact the resident’s guardian to seek permission to administer the medication (resident files numbered 1 through 3);

c. The prescribing physician’s directions for monitoring medications used by the resident (resident files numbered 1 through 3);

d. The disposition of a resident’s prescription medication upon discharge (resident files numbered 1 and 2).

Rule Violated: Minnesota Rules, part 2960.0080, subpart 11, items D, E, and F.

16. Violation: One of two resident files reviewed for rules governing discharge procedures did not meet requirements. The license holder did not have documentation to demonstrate that they returned all of the resident’s personal property to them upon discharge (resident file numbered 2).

Rule Violated: Minnesota Rules, part 2960.0090, subpart 3.

17. Violation: Three of six personnel files reviewed for rules governing staff qualifications did not meet requirements. The file did not contain documentation to demonstrate staff met the following qualifications:

a. The staff had been trained in gender-based needs and issues (personnel files numbered 1, 4, and 5);

b. The staff member is at least 21 years old (personnel files numbered 4 and 5); and

c. The professional licensure is current (personnel files numbered 4 and 5).

Rule Violated: Minnesota Rules, parts 2960.0100, subpart 6, items B and C, and 2960.0150, subpart 2.

Immediate corrective action required

You must immediately correct the violations cited above. Submit documentation to your licensor within 30 days from when you received this order explaining how you have corrected the violations. If you fail to demonstrate substantial compliance with Children’s Residential Facilities requirements or with the terms of your conditional license that are provided below, DHS may take an additional licensing action, including revocation, against your license.

Please submit documentation via the following methods:

1. By secure email to: hannah.horsch@state.mn.us; or

2. By mail to:

Commissioner, Department of Human Services
ATTN: Hannah Horsch
Licensing Division
PO Box 64242
St. Paul, MN 55164-0242

Additionally, DHS will not approve a request to license another program setting from the date of this order until the time your conditional license expires.

CONDITIONAL LICENSE TERMS

In addition to the Children’s Residential Facility licensing rules and statutes, you are required to comply with the following terms:

1. Within 14 days of receipt of this order, you must notify current residents and all parties who refer residents to your program of the conditional status of your license. The notification must be approved by DHS Licensing prior to being sent to residents and all other parties. Therefore, the draft notice must be submitted to DHS for approval within 10 days of receipt of this order. The notification must specify the length of time of the conditional status of your license, the reasons your license was placed on conditional status, and include either a copy of the Order of Conditional License or an offer to provide a copy upon request.

While the license is on conditional status, you must notify new residents and referral sources that the license is on conditional status before they begin receiving treatment services. The notification to new residents must specify the length of time of the conditional status of your license, the reasons the license was made conditional, and it must include either a copy of the Order of Conditional License or an offer to provide a copy of the order upon request. A copy of the notice with resident and legal representative(s) signature must be maintained in the resident file.

Within 30 days of receipt of this order, you must submit to the DHS Licensing Division, a copy of the notice and a list of all referral sources that received the notice.

2. Within 14 days of receipt of this order, you must submit to DHS Licensing a resume for any proposed Compliance Officer that addresses each qualification listed below. The license holder agrees to ensure that the program’s Compliance Officer will not hold the position of Program Director. If providing direct care services, the Compliance Officer may only allocate 50 percent of their working hours to direct care services. The resume must address each qualification listed below. The program’s Compliance Officer must have the following minimum qualifications and must be approved by the program’s DHS Licensor:

a. 2 years of professional experience in the following:

1) A regulatory or compliance position; or

2) Providing direct care services to youth in a children’s residential facility; and

b. Working knowledge of Minnesota Statutes, Chapter 245A, Human Services Licensing and Minnesota Rules, chapter 2960.

3. Within 30 days of the receipt of this order, you must implement the DHS Licensing self-monitoring plan that ensures an ongoing, systematic approach for monitoring compliance with applicable licensing laws, rules, and statutes. The Compliance Officer must conduct, within the first quarter, a review of the license holder’s policies and procedures to ensure compliance with Minnesota Statutes, Chapters 245A and Minnesota Rules, chapter 2960. This includes an assessment of staff implementing the policies and procedures.

Documentation requirements of the results of the monthly reviews are outlined in the DHS self-monitoring plan. Documentation of the discrepancies found when conducting the reviews and the corrective actions taken must be submitted to the program’s DHS Licensor quarterly, and continuing every three months thereafter, on the 15th of month following the quarter, for 2 years from the effective date of the conditional license or otherwise determined by DHS licensing.

4. Within 30 days receipt of this order, the Authorized Agent, Program Director and Compliance Officer (upon hire) must read Minnesota Statutes, chapters 245A and 245C, and Minnesota Rules, parts 2960.0010 through 2960.0220 and contact your licensor to arrange a competency evaluation to demonstrate your knowledge of the applicable Minnesota Statutes and Rules.

5. Within 30 days receipt of this order, you must submit to DHS a personnel training plan that outlines all topics staff are trained on, the contents of each training, who provides the training, how the training is delivered, and how staff competency is evaluated. You must include a mock personnel file that contains orientation and training checklists required of group residential facilities.

6. Within 45 days of receipt of this order, you must submit a fully revised and compliant policy and procedure manual in electronic format to DHS for review and approval. The policy and procedure manual must include all of the necessary policies and procedures the license holder requires to maintain compliance with the licensing requirements under Minnesota Statutes and Minnesota Rules. The policy and procedure manual must explicitly identify the policies and procedures applicable to the licensing requirements that govern the children’s residential facility license.

Within 14 days of the approval from DHS Licensing, the license holder must train all program staff to their duties in implementing the program’s policies and procedures and submit a training record to DHS Licensing demonstrating the completion of the training.

7. Throughout the duration of the conditional license, any changes to program leadership staff must be reported to and approved by DHS Licensing, before the staff person assumes responsibilities of the role. Leadership staff includes the Authorized Agent, Program Director, Sensitive Information Person (background studies), and Compliance Officer.

YOUR RIGHT TO REQUEST RECONSIDERATION

You have the right to request reconsideration of this order and the cited violations. Your request must:

· Be in writing

· Clearly state that you are requesting reconsideration of the conditional license

· List each citation you are challenging and identify what is inaccurate or incomplete about the information in the order

· Supply information that is accurate or more complete

· State why you believe your license should not be on a conditional status

· Be made before the deadlines provided below

If you are mailing your request, it must be sent by certified mail and postmarked within 10 calendar days from when you received this order. If you do not meet this deadline, you lose your right to request reconsideration. The timeline to appeal began when you received this order. Please send it to:

Commissioner, Department of Human Services

Office of Inspector General

Legal Counsel’s Office

Attn: Licensing Legal Unit

PO Box 64953

St. Paul, MN 55164-0953

If your request is being personally delivered, it must be received by DHS within 10 calendar days from when you received this order. Please bring it to:

Commissioner, Department of Human Services

Office of Inspector General

Legal Counsel’s Office

Attn: Licensing Legal Unit

444 Lafayette Road North

St. Paul, MN 55155

Conditional license stayed pending reconsideration

If you request reconsideration within the timeframes described above, the terms of the conditional license will not take effect until a decision is issued by DHS. If the conditional license is affirmed on reconsideration, the terms would take effect on the date of the reconsideration decision and run for two years from that date. You continue to be required to comply with all Children’s Residential Facilities laws and rules.

Legal authority for this licensing action

· This action is taken under Minnesota Statutes, section 245A.06, subdivision 1.

· Group residential settings are required to follow Minnesota Rules, parts 2960.0130 – 2960.0220.

· The timeline to request reconsideration of the order is provided in Minnesota Statutes, section 245A.06, subdivision 4.

· If a license holder files a timely reconsideration request, the terms of the conditional license are stayed pending a decision by DHS under Minnesota Statutes, section 245A.06, subdivision 4.

· Minnesota Statutes, section 245A.06, subdivision 3 states that DHS may impose additional licensing actions against a license holder that does not correct the violations cited in a conditional license order.

Questions

If you have any further questions regarding this matter, you may contact Katie Leuer, Supervisor, at 651-431-6259.

Sincerely,

Paula Halverson, Unit Manager

Licensing Division

Office of Inspector General

PO Box 64242 • Saint Paul, Minnesota • 55164-0242 • An Equal Opportunity and Veteran Friendly Employer

reports.summary: The Department of Human Services (DHS) is placing your license to provide at The Freedom Ranch, 16966 County Road 2 Brainerd, MN, on conditional status for two years, beginning December 31, 2024. This means you must meet certain conditions to maintain your license, detailed below. This order is based on your noncompliance with Children’s Residential Facilities licensing requirements.

reports.raw_content:
10/23/2024                    CERTIFIED MAIL

Vicki Leckie, Authorized Agent

Rising Hope Foundation

15959 Blue Heron Drive

Brainerd MN 56401-6098

License Number: 1116745 (CRF)

Report Number: 202408794

ORDER TO PAY A FINE

Dear Vicki Leckie:

The Department of Human Services (DHS) is ordering you to pay a fine of $600 for three licensing violations at your Children’s Residential Facility, The Freedom Ranch, located at 16966 County Road 2, Brainerd MN 56401. Details of our findings are provided below. Our next steps and your options are also detailed.

REASON FOR FINE

1. Supervision Violation

As a result of a licensing investigation conducted on October 11, 2024, a DHS licensor determined that the license holder failed to supervise children in care.

On October 6, 8 and 9, 2024, there was not at least one staff person present and awake at all times in the facility when residents are normally asleep. The license holder instead had a staff member in an attached apartment unit asleep during the shifts.

Fine: $200 for each occurrence (October 6, 8 and 9, 2024).

Corrective Action Required: Immediately and on an ongoing basis, the license holder must ensure the supervision of children meets all applicable requirements.

Rule Violated: Minnesota Rules, part 2960.0150, subpart 3, item G.

Billing and Payment of the Fine

DHS will send you an invoice for the $600 fine. Payment must be made as directed on the invoice.

If you request a contested case hearing, as described below, do not pay the fine at this time. Following the contested case hearing, the Commissioner of DHS will issue a final order on this matter.

Please note, you may not avoid payment of this fine by closing, selling, or otherwise transferring the license to a third party. If this occurs, each controlling individual is personally and jointly responsible for payment. If you do not pay the fine on or before the date specified on the invoice and you did not request a contested case hearing, as described below, the Commissioner may issue a second fine, may not issue or reissue a license, or may suspend the license until the license holder pays the fine.

YOUR RIGHT TO APPEAL

You have the right to appeal the fine. Your request must be in writing and clearly state that you are requesting a contested case hearing for this matter. Your request must be made before the deadlines provided below. If you do not meet this deadline, you lose your right to an administrative appeal. The timeline to appeal began when you received this order.

If you are mailing your request, it must be sent by certified mail and postmarked within 10 calendar days from when you receive this order. Please send it to:

Commissioner, Department of Human Services

Office of Inspector General

Legal Counsel’s Office

Attention: Licensing Legal Unit

PO Box 64953

St. Paul, MN 55164-0953

If your request is being personally delivered, it must be received by DHS within 10 calendar days from when you receive this order. Please bring it to:

Commissioner, Department of Human Services

Office of Inspector General

Legal Counsel’s Office

Attention: Licensing Legal Unit

444 Lafayette Road North

St. Paul, MN 55155

Upon DHS’ receipt of your timely appeal, your case would be scheduled for a contested case hearing in front of an Administrative Law Judge. Following this hearing, the Commissioner of DHS will issue a final order.

Legal representation at the contested case hearing

You do not need a lawyer to appeal. However, a lawyer can help you with your appeal. The state or county will not get you a lawyer and will not pay for a lawyer. If you cannot afford a lawyer, you may be able to get free legal advice or help with your appeal. To find out if free help is available, contact: Volunteer Lawyers Network at 612-752-6677; Central Minnesota Legal Services at 612-332-8151; Southern Minnesota Legal Services at 651-222-4731; or go to www.lawhelpmn.org to find a local legal services program that may be able to help you.

You can also find information on contested cases from the Office of Administrative Hearings website at https://mn.gov/oah/self-help. Click on Administrative Law Overview, then click on Administrative Law Contested Case Hearing Guide for a list of frequently asked questions.

Legal authority for this licensing action

· This action is taken under Minnesota Statutes, section 245A.07, subdivision 1, paragraph (a), which describes under which conditions DHS may impose a fine against a license.

· DHS may impose a $200 fine for each health, safety, or supervision licensing violation under Minnesota Statutes, section 245A.07, subdivision 3, paragraph (c)(4)(iv).

· Minnesota Statutes, section 245A.07, subdivision 3, paragraph (c)(5) states that license holders may not avoid payment of a fine by closing, selling, or transferring a license.

· Minnesota Statutes, section 245A.04, subdivision 7, paragraph (d)(4), states that DHS shall not issue or reissue a license if the applicant, license holder, or controlling individual has an outstanding debt related to a license fee, licensing fine, or settlement agreement for which payment is delinquent.

· Minnesota Statutes, section 245A.07, subdivision 3, paragraph (c)(2), states that that the license holder shall pay the fine assessed on or before the payment date specified, and if the license holder does not do so the commissioner may issue a second fine or suspend the license until the license holder complies.

· License holders have a right to appeal licensing actions and request a contested case hearing, under Minnesota Statutes, chapter 14 and Minnesota Rules, parts 1400.8505 to 1400.8612.

Questions

If you have any further questions regarding this matter, you may contact Katie Leuer, Supervisor, at 651-431-6259.

Sincerely,

Paula Halverson, Unit Manager

Licensing Division

Office of Inspector General

PO Box 64242 • Saint Paul, Minnesota • 55164-0242 • An Equal Opportunity and Veteran Friendly Employer
